name: Alanine
description: Small simple side chain; one of the most common amino acids and a flexible building block in protein cores.

name: Arginine
description: Carries a positive charge; common in active sites and a precursor to the signaling molecule nitric oxide.

name: Asparagine
description: Amide side chain; a frequent attachment point for sugars (N-linked glycosylation) on secreted proteins.

name: Aspartate
description: Carries a negative charge; common in enzyme active sites and metal-binding pockets.

name: Cysteine
description: Sulfur-containing; pairs with other cysteines to form disulfide bonds that staple a protein's shape together.

name: Glutamate
description: Carries a negative charge; abundant on protein surfaces and the brain's main excitatory neurotransmitter.

name: Glutamine
description: Amide side chain; the most abundant free amino acid in blood and a key carrier of nitrogen.

name: Glycine
description: The smallest amino acid, just a hydrogen side chain, giving the protein backbone extra flexibility.

name: Histidine
description: Imidazole side chain that switches charge near neutral pH, making it ideal for enzyme catalysis.
deficiency_note: Essential in the diet; deficiency is uncommon but can contribute to anemia and skin problems.

name: Isoleucine
description: A branched-chain amino acid that packs into the water-avoiding cores of proteins.
deficiency_note: Essential branched-chain amino acid; prolonged deficiency can cause muscle wasting and fatigue.

name: Leucine
description: The most common amino acid; a branched-chain residue central to protein folding and muscle signaling.
deficiency_note: Essential branched-chain amino acid; deficiency can impair muscle maintenance and growth.

name: Lysine
description: Long positively charged side chain; a frequent target for chemical tags that switch protein activity on and off.
deficiency_note: Essential in the diet; deficiency can cause fatigue, slowed growth, and impaired immune function.

name: Methionine
description: Sulfur-containing; encoded by the start codon, so nearly every protein begins with it.
deficiency_note: Essential in the diet; deficiency can impair growth and lead to fat accumulation in the liver.

name: Phenylalanine
description: Large aromatic ring; packs into protein cores and is the body's precursor to tyrosine.
deficiency_note: Essential in the diet; deficiency is rare but can cause confusion and low energy.

name: Proline
description: Its ring locks the backbone into kinks, often breaking helices and forming tight turns.

name: Serine
description: Small hydroxyl side chain; a common site for regulatory phosphorylation and a player in enzyme catalysis.

name: Threonine
description: Hydroxyl side chain; like serine, a frequent target of regulatory phosphorylation.
deficiency_note: Essential in the diet; deficiency can impair immune function and digestion.

name: Tryptophan
description: The largest amino acid; its bulky ring is rare and serves as a precursor to serotonin and niacin.
deficiency_note: Essential in the diet; severe deficiency contributes to pellagra-like symptoms and low mood.

name: Tyrosine
description: Aromatic with a hydroxyl group; phosphorylation here is a key on/off switch in cell signaling.

name: Valine
description: A branched-chain amino acid that fills the hydrophobic cores of proteins.
deficiency_note: Essential branched-chain amino acid; deficiency can cause muscle weakness and neurological symptoms.
